title_en: Modes of Transmission: Hearing, Reading, and Written Authorization
title_ar: طرق التحمل والأداء: السماع والقراءة والإجازة
content_en:
A substantial portion of Al-Kifayah fi Ilm al-Riwayah is devoted to the technical question of how a narrator legitimately acquires and then transmits a hadith. Al-Khatib al-Baghdadi identifies eight recognized modes of transmission (turuq al-tahammul wa al-ada'), each with its own conditions, ranks, and appropriate citation formulas. This systematic treatment was foundational for all subsequent hadith methodology.

Al-Sama': Direct Oral Hearing

The highest form of reception is sama' — directly hearing the teacher recite the hadith from memory. The student who attends a dictation session (imla') or hears the shaykh narrate to a group is in the most advantageous position with respect to transmission reliability. When citing a hadith received this way, the narrator uses formulas such as hadathana (he narrated to us) or akhbarana (he informed us), which signal direct audition.

Al-Khatib examines the conditions for valid sama': the student must be paying attention, must be within audible range, and must be in a state of mental clarity. He discusses whether children (below the age of discernment) can validly hear hadiths, noting that most scholars accepted the transmissions of those who heard at ages five or above, provided they demonstrated adequate comprehension. This discussion has practical consequences because many of the greatest transmitters of the early generations heard hadiths as young children.

Al-Qira'ah: Reading to the Teacher

Qira'ah ala al-shaykh (reading to the teacher) is the second mode, in which the student reads from a written text and the teacher confirms or corrects what is read. This method, essentially a form of oral examination, was widely used and ranked very close to sama' by most scholars. The appropriate citation formula is qara'tu ala fulani (I read to so-and-so) or akhbarana qira'atan alayhi (he informed me through my reading to him).

Al-Khatib addresses a practical debate: if the student reads and the teacher dozes off during part of the session, are the hadiths heard while the teacher was asleep validly transmitted? He presents various scholarly positions, generally favoring the view that brief inattention does not invalidate the transmission of a properly conducted reading session, but that the student should note any portions where the teacher was clearly not attending.

Al-Ijazah: Written or Verbal Authorization

Ijazah (authorization or license) is the practice of a scholar granting another person permission to transmit his corpus of hadiths or certain books without the recipient having heard them directly. Al-Khatib devotes careful attention to the conditions under which ijazah is valid. He distinguishes between ijazah to a named individual, ijazah to an unspecified group, and ijazah to those not yet born — the last being a practice he considers problematic.

The question of whether ijazah constitutes genuine transmission at all was debated by the scholars. Al-Khatib argues that ijazah is a legitimate mode of transmission when it is specific and properly documented, though it is ranked below sama' and qira'ah in terms of reliability. He connects ijazah to the broader practice of giving books as gifts or sending them by messenger (munawala), which represents a further degree of indirect authorization.

Kitabah and Other Modes

Al-Khatib also covers kitabah (written correspondence), in which a scholar sends written hadiths to a student; ijazah al-munawala (authorization with a physical transmission of the text); wajada (finding a hadith in a book without tracing its transmission); and wasiyyah (bequeathing one's hadith books). Each mode has its appropriate citation formulas and its position in the ranking of transmission reliability. This systematic taxonomy was adopted, refined, and transmitted by every subsequent generation of hadith methodologists.
